task.prompt: Write a function `normalize_ranges(ranges)` that normalizes a list of integer ranges.

tests.code:
items = [(3, 1), (7, 7)]
out = normalize_ranges(items)
assert items == [(3, 1), (7, 7)]
assert out == [(1, 3), (7, 7)]

gold_spec.goal: Normalize closed integer ranges into sorted, non-overlapping ranges.

inputs.name: ranges
inputs.description: closed integer ranges; each pair may be written in either order

outputs.name: result
outputs.description: normalized closed ranges sorted by start

preconditions: Each input range contains exactly two integers.
postconditions: Treat every input pair as a closed range from min(start, end) through max(start, end).
Sort ranges by their normalized start.
Merge ranges that overlap or touch, so (1, 2) and (3, 4) become (1, 4).
Do not mutate the input list.
edge_cases: Empty input returns an empty list.
A reversed range such as (5, 3) is normalized to (3, 5).
Singleton ranges such as (4, 4) are valid closed ranges.
acceptance_criteria: The output is sorted by start.
No two output ranges overlap or touch.
The output covers exactly the same integer points as the normalized input ranges.

known_weakness.description: The raw prompt does not say whether ranges are closed, whether reversed endpoints are valid, or whether touching ranges should merge.
known_weakness.naive_spec_gap: missing closed-interval, reversed-endpoint, adjacency, and non-mutation semantics
